svg.text: LATIN (LATINA) / ITALIAN (ITALIANO)
INDO-EUROPEAN · ITALIC BRANCH · HISTORY & LEGACY
PLATE 22D ALT · HISTORY & CULTURAL LEGACY
— LATIN: HISTORY & INFLUENCE —
FROM VILLAGE DIALECT TO WORLD LANGUAGE
753 BCE (trad.): Rome founded · Latin a minor Italic dialect of Latium
3rd–1st c. BCE: Republican expansion → Latin spreads throughout Italy then empire
~27 BCE–476 CE: Roman Empire · Latin as administrative language from Britain to Mesopotamia
VULGAR LATIN: WHAT PEOPLE ACTUALLY SPOKE
Classical Latin: prestige written register — few spoke it natively
Vulgar Latin: colloquial spoken form, varied by region, lost noun cases early
Graffiti at Pompeii shows Vulgar Latin in action: simplified grammar, new words
THE ROMANCE FAMILY: LATIN'S LIVING DESCENDANTS
Spanish ~490M · Portuguese ~260M · French ~280M
Italian ~65M · Romanian ~25M · Catalan ~10M · Occitan ~1M
All descend from Vulgar Latin, not Classical Latin — the spoken form survived
LATIN'S SECOND LIFE: MEDIEVAL & RENAISSANCE
Church Latin (Ecclesiastical): Mass, theology, liturgy 400–1962 CE
Medieval Latin: science, law, philosophy — lingua franca of scholars
Renaissance: Classical Latin revived as ideal — Cicero imitated over medieval style
Latin as scientific nomenclature: still used in biology, medicine, law today
LATIN IN ENGLISH: ~60% OF ENGLISH VOCABULARY
Direct: via French after 1066 (Norman Conquest) · aqua-, terra-, vita-
Legal: habeas corpus, in absentia, mens rea, prima facie
Scientific: Homo sapiens, Tyrannosaurus rex, Rosa canina (dog rose)
ITALIAN (ITALIANO) — HISTORY & LITERARY LEGACY
— ITALIAN: THE LANGUAGE DANTE BUILT —
BEFORE STANDARD ITALIAN: ITALIAN DIALECTS
Before ~1300: no "Italian" — dozens of mutually unintelligible Romance dialects
Venetian, Sicilian, Neapolitan, Lombard — each with own literature
DANTE ALIGHIERI (1265–1321): THE FATHER OF ITALIAN
La Divina Commedia (Inferno, Purgatorio, Paradiso): written in Tuscan vernacular
Chose volgare (common tongue) over Latin to reach a wider audience
Boccaccio's Decameron, Petrarch's Canzoniere: established Tuscan as prestige model
RISORGIMENTO & NATIONAL LANGUAGE (19TH C.)
Italian unification 1861: a country unified before a common language
Manzoni's I Promessi Sposi (The Betrothed, 1827/1840) modeled standard Italian
~1861: only 2–3% of Italians spoke standard Italian; rest spoke dialects
Television (RAI, 1954) and compulsory schooling unified the spoken language
ITALIAN IN THE ARTS: A UNIVERSAL LANGUAGE
Opera: Verdi · Puccini · Rossini — Italian the language of classical opera worldwide
Music terms in every language: piano · forte · allegro · soprano · crescendo
Cuisine vocabulary: pasta · pizza · espresso · prosciutto — Italian exports
THE CONTINUOUS THREAD
Latin → Vulgar Latin → Old Italian → Dante's Tuscan → Modern Italian
~2,000 years of continuous Latin-family speech in the same peninsula
SURVIVAL PHRASE
Siamo tutti umani — We are all human (cf. Latin: Omnes homines sumus)
